q: Molecular biology is the branch of biology that seeks to understand the molecular basis of biological activity in and between cells, including biomolecular synthesis, modification, mechanisms, and interactions.
The study of chemical, physical structures, and functions of biological macromolecules is known as molecular biology.
Nucleic acids are the main information-carrying molecules of the cell which direct the process of protein synthesis by determining the inherited characteristics of every living thing.
The two main classes of nucleic acids are deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA).
DNA constitutes the genetic material in all free-living organisms and all viruses.
RNA is the genetic material of certain viruses and plays an important role in certain processes such as the making of proteins.
DNA is a double helix structure consisting of two polynucleotide strands twine around each other.
Each nucleotide in DNA is composed of a six-carbon deoxyribose sugar, a phosphate group, and a nitrogen-containing base.
The components of a nucleotide in DNA are all linked together by covalent bonds.
The purine bases are adenine (A) and guanine (G), and they have two fused rings in their chemical structures.
The pyrimidine bases are cytosine (C) and thymine (T), and they have a single ring in their chemical structure.
Each strand of DNA forms a backbone of alternating phosphate groups and sugars.
The bases of each nucleotide in DNA are attached to each sugar and protrude inward at regular intervals along each strand.
There is a constant total distance between the sugar-phosphate backbones in a DNA molecule.
The two strands of a DNA molecule are identical to each other.
A purine molecule is always paired with a pyrimidine molecule in a DNA double helix.
Specifically, adenine (A) always pairs with thymine (T) and guanine (G) always pairs with cytosine (C).
Hydrogen bonds link each complementary base pair in the DNA molecule.
Adenine and Thymine are joined by three hydrogen bonds, while Cytosine and Guanine are joined by two hydrogen bonds.
The two strands of a DNA molecule are antiparallel, meaning the sugar molecules are oriented differently.
One strand of DNA lies in $5'$ to $3'$ direction and another strand in $3'$ to $5'$ direction.
The phosphate group is on the $5'$ carbon, and the OH group is on the $3'$ carbon of the deoxyribose sugar.
By convention, the sequence of a DNA strand is always written in the $3'$ to $5'$ direction.
RNA is a polymer of nucleotides containing the bases adenine (A), uracil (U), cytosine (C), and guanine (G).
Unlike DNA, RNA is always single-stranded and cannot form complementary base pairing within the same molecule.
Messenger RNA (mRNA) acts as the template for translation.
Transfer RNA (tRNA) carries specific amino acid to mRNA at ribosome.
Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) reads the codon on mRNA.
DNA is the hereditary material responsible for passing genetic information from cell to cell and generation to generation.
There are around $3.2$ billion base pairs in the DNA of a typical mammalian cell.
The variety of sequences or bases along the length of a DNA molecule provides the genetic diversity within living organisms.
Base pairing leads to DNA being able to replicate and to transfer information to mRNA.
DNA replication is regarded as semiconservative because each of the resulting two molecules consists of two new strands.
Replication begins at special sites called origins of replication, where replication bubbles form.
Helicase enzymes unwind the double helix and unzip the two parental strands by breaking hydrogen bonds.
Single-stranded binding proteins act as scaffolding, holding the two strands apart during replication.
Topoisomerase increases the tension on the tight helix as it unwinds.
DNA polymerase III can initiate the synthesis of a new DNA strand starting from individual nucleotides.
The preexisting chain required for DNA polymerase III consists of an RNA primer produced by the enzyme primase.
DNA polymerase can only add complementary nucleotides to the free $3'$ end of the primer, never to the $5'$ end.
A new DNA strand can elongate in both the $5'$ to $3'$ and $3'$ to $5'$ directions.
The leading strand is formed towards the replication fork in a continuous and linear fashion.
The lagging strand is formed in direction away from the replication fork, in a series of segments called Okazaki fragments.
Each Okazaki fragment is about 100-200 nucleotides long and they are joined by the enzyme DNA ligase.
DNA polymerase I carries out mismatch repair, a kind of proofreading that corrects errors.
Damaged regions of DNA are excised out by DNA nuclease.
To protect against possible loss of genes, eukaryotes have special nonsense nucleotide sequences (TAAGGG) called telomeres at the ends of chromosomes.
Telomeres get longer every time DNA replicates to prevent aging.
DNA ligase joins Okazaki fragments of lagging strand and joins the $3'$ end of DNA that replaces primer to the rest of leading strand DNA.
DNA polymerase I removes RNA nucleotides of primer from $5'$ end and replaces them with DNA nucleotides.
Genetic code is a set of rules for determining how genetic information in the form of a nucleotide sequence is converted to an amino acid sequence of a protein.
There are only four nucleotides in RNA (A, U, G, and C), but 20 different kinds of amino acids.
A one-to-one relationship between nucleotides and amino acids would provide enough combinations for 20 amino acids.
A doublet code using two nucleotides per amino acid would provide 16 possible combinations.
The minimum combination of the four nucleotides was a triplet code, which could produce 64 possible combinations.
The triplet hypothesis proposed that the genetic code consists of a combination of three nucleotides, called a codon.
AUG is a start codon and also codes for the amino acid methionine.
UAA, UAG, and UGA are initiator codons for protein synthesis.
The genetic code is always interpreted in terms of the mRNA codon rather than the nucleotide sequence of the DNA.
The genetic code is redundant, meaning that more than one codon can code for the same amino acid.
There are exactly four codons that do not code for any amino acid and serve as 'stop' signals.
The genetic code is continuous, meaning it reads as a series of three-letter codons without spaces, punctuation, or overlap.
A shift of one or two nucleotides in either direction can alter the codon groupings and result in an incorrect amino acid sequence.
The genetic code is nearly universal, meaning almost all organisms build proteins with the same genetic code.
A gene that is taken from one kind of organism and inserted into another kind of organism will produce a different protein due to variations in the genetic code.
The two steps in protein synthesis are transcription and translation.
Transcription involves the production of a protein with an amino acid sequence based on the nucleic acid sequence of the mRNA.
The central dogma of molecular biology stated that genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to Protein.
Transcription occurs in the nucleus of the cell.
There are three defined stages in the transcription process: initiation, elongation, and termination.
For each gene, both strands of the double-stranded DNA molecule are transcribed to produce mRNA.
The DNA strand that is transcribed is called the antisense strand or template strand.
The sense strand or coding strand has the same sequence as the product mRNA, with thymine instead of uracil.
During elongation, the RNA polymerase complex synthesizes a strand of mRNA that is complementary to the template strand of DNA in $5'$ to $3'$ direction.
Termination occurs when the RNA polymerase complexes reach the termination signal and detach from the DNA strand.
The newly synthesized mRNA is called mature mRNA before any modifications occur.
Modifications convert pre-mRNA to mature mRNA through the addition of a $5'$ cap and a $3'$ poly-A tail.
Removal of introns (non-coding regions) and joining of exons (coding regions) is part of the mRNA modification process.
A poly-A tail consisting of 100-200 adenine nucleotides is added after transcription.
Splicing involves snRNA forming base-pairs with the $5'$ end of an intron and at the branch site to form a spliceosome.
Translation occurs in the cytoplasm and involves the conversion of a sequence of nucleotides into a sequence of amino acids.
There are two subunits in the ribosome: the small subunit and the large subunit.
There are three binding sites for tRNA on the large subunit of ribosome: A site, P site and E site.
Each tRNA carries the specific amino acid at a time at its acceptor stem.
The anticodon on tRNA connects to the specific codon on the mRNA.
In the initiation phase of translation, the first tRNA that binds to the codon is the initiator tRNA with its UAC anticodon.
During elongation, a tRNA with an attached polypeptide is in the A site, then a peptide bond is formed and the next amino acid enters the P site.
The termination phase of translation begins when a stop codon on the mRNA is reached.
A protein called a release factor cleaves (cuts) the polypeptide from the last tRNA during termination.
Scientists can use genetically modified organisms (GMOs) to produce medications and to treat genetic disorders.
Genetic engineering is the alteration of the genetic material of an organism in a specific manner.
Transgenic describes an organism that is produced from the introduction of foreign DNA into its genome.
Recombinant DNA is a method of transferring a gene from one organism into the genome of another organism of the same species.
A vector, such as a plasmid of bacteria, is needed to insert the recombinant gene into a target cell.
Vitamin A deficiency can cause blindness and immune deficiency syndrome.
The genetically modified rice called Golden Rice contains the orange pigment beta-carotene, which can be converted into vitamin A.
The genes that give golden rice its ability to make beta-carotene come from maize and Erwinia bacteria.
One benefit of genetic engineering of crops is increased resistance to a range of pests and pathogens.
Plants are being engineered to produce medicinal products such as human growth hormone and clotting factors.
One type of antibody made by corn can deliver radioisotopes to tumour cells.
The production of transgenic animals is a much simpler and less controversial process than the production of transgenic plants.
To produce transgenic animals, a foreign gene is inserted into the genome of an animal oocyte (egg) that is then fertilized.
Transgenic animals can be used to produce pharmaceutical products such as human proteins by using a recombinant plasmid vector.
The milk of female transgenic offspring can contain human growth protein.
Benefits of GM animals include increased production of a particular product and production of organs for transplantation (xero-transplantation).
